coredata.dc:title: Thiomersal determination in vaccines using graphene quantum dots-TiO2 nanocomposite to promote temporally selective photo-production of Hg cold vapor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part B: Atomic Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Miranda-Andrades, Jarol R.
Pedrozo-Penãfiel, Marlin J.
Mendes, Joseane A.
Aucelio, Ricardo Queiroz
coredata.dc:description: Determination of thiomersal was made by the temporally selective photo-degradation, mediated by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and TiO2 nanoparticles (GQDs-TiO2) composite, producing the Hg0 quantified by atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) using a multipass detection cell. The UV photo-reactor was directly adapted to the mercury dedicated spectrometer with samples mixed, into quartz tube, to a solution (buffered at pH 4.0) containing formic acid (1% v/v) and the dispersed GQDs-TiO2 (0.18 mg L−1). Hg0 temporal profile from thiomersal was detected after 16 min of UV exposure. Different kinetics of Hg0 releasing allowed speciation as the Hg0 from other mercurial species were produced at different times. Analytical linear range was from 15 ng L−1 (LOD) up to 1000 ng L−1. Temporal separation of Hg0 formation was confirmed by gas chromatography coupled to atomic fluorescence spectrometry, also indicating that thiomersal is firstly degraded to ethylmercury before turning into Hg2+ and then into Hg0. The method was applied in the determination of thiomersal in vaccines and residual levels of Hg2+ and ethylmercury were also detected.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thiomersal
Graphene quantum dots
TiO2 nanoparticles
Photocatalysis
Cold vapor atomic spectrometry

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part B: Atomic Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of thiomersal using CV-AAS with photo-reaction mediated by GQDs−TiO2
Mercury speciation using a mercury analyzer with atomic fluorescence detection
Study of photodegradation using the GC-CV-AFS system with propylation of mercurial species
Optimization of photo-degradation adjusting pH and GQDs-TiO2 content for CV − AAS measurements
UV exposure time for CV-AAS measurements
Evaluation of the relative proportion between GQDs-TiO2 and thiomersal in CV − AAS measurements
Transformation of thiomersal into ethylmercury study using GC − CV − AFS
Evaluation of interferences using CV − AAS

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Production of Hg0 from thiomersal (a 2 μg L−1) under UV: (a) Blank, (b) 200 μL of TiO2 nanoparticle dispersion; (c) 200 μL GQDs-TiO2 dispersion. Dispersions consisting of 30 mg of nanomaterial in 5 mL of water. B) Chromatogram showing the formation of ethylmercury from a 300 pg thiomersal in the presence of GQDs-TiO2 over the UV irradiation times: a) 0 min, b) 5 min, c) 10 min, d) 15 min, e) 25 min.; The formation of Hg2+ over the irradiation times f) 15 min; g) 25 min; h) 30 min.; The formation of Hg0 over the irradiation times i) residual; j) 15 min; k) 25 min; l) 30 min.

Hg0 measured after photo-degradation of thiomersal (2 μg L−1): A) using different volumes (10–400 μL) of the original GQDs-TiO2 dispersion (6 mg L−1) at original pH (a) and at pH 4.0 (b); B) under UV exposure time in the presence of 150 μL GQDs−TiO2 (0.18 mg L−1) at pH 4.0 (a) and at pH 4.0 in the presence of 1% formic acid and 1% (v/v) H2O2 (b).

A) chromatographic profile with AFS detection of the products from photo-degradation of thiomersal (300 ng L−1): ethylmercury at tR = 4.85 min; Hg2+ at tR = 7.78 min and Hg at tR = 0.36 min. UV radiation times: 0 min (a); 2 min (b); 4 min (c); 6 min (d); 8 min (e); 10 min (f); 12 min (g); 15 min (h, i, j). B) ln (ct/c0) plot for initial concentration (c0) of thiomersal at 300 ng L− with ct being concentrations measured at different irradiation times. Reaction system with 150 μL GQDs-TiO2 original dispersion (0.18 mg L−1) in the original pH of the mixture (about 5).

A) Hg0 absorbance time profiles in function of UV exposure time from mercurial solutions containing dispersed GQDs-TiO2 (0.18 mg L−1) at pH 4.0 in the presence of formic acid (1% v/v): A) Hg2+ at 1 μg L−1; B) ethylmercury at 1 μg L−1; C) methylmercury at 1 μg L−1and D) thiomersal at 2 μg L−1.

Hg0 temporal profiles from increasing concentrations of thiomersal using CV-AAS with a multipass cell. (a) 30 ng L−1; (b) 50 ng L−1; (c) 75 ng L−1; (d) 100 ngL−1; (e)140 ng L−1; (f) 175 ng L−1; (g) 200 ng L−1; (h) 250 ng L−1; (i) 300 ng L−1; (j) 350 ng L−1; (k)500 ng L−1; (l) 680 ng L−1; (m) 800 ng L−1; e (n)1000 ng L−1 (analytical curve inserted).
Parameters for thiomersal photo-degradation mediated by GQDs-TiO2 with detection by CV-AAS.

row.entry: 253 nm (secondary lines in the range of 296 to 313 nm) and 24 W
0.18 mg L−1 (from a 150 μL of nanomaterial dispersion at 6 mg mL−1).

head.ce:title: Thiomersal determination in vaccines using graphene quantum dots-TiO2 nanocomposite to promote temporally selective photo-production of Hg cold vapor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Pontifical Catholic University of Rio de Janeiro (PUC-Rio), Rio de Janeiro, 22451-900, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pontifical Catholic University of Rio de Janeiro (PUC-Rio)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Pontifical Catholic University of Rio de Janeiro (PUC-Rio), Rio de Janeiro, 22451-900, Brazil.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Determination of thiomersal was made by the temporally selective photo-degradation, mediated by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and TiO2 nanoparticles (GQDs-TiO2) composite, producing the Hg0 quantified by atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) using a multipass detection cell. The UV photo-reactor was directly adapted to the mercury dedicated spectrometer with samples mixed, into quartz tube, to a solution (buffered at pH 4.0) containing formic acid (1% v/v) and the dispersed GQDs-TiO2 (0.18 mg L−1). Hg0 temporal profile from thiomersal was detected after 16 min of UV exposure. Different kinetics of Hg0 releasing allowed speciation as the Hg0 from other mercurial species were produced at different times. Analytical linear range was from 15 ng L−1 (LOD) up to 1000 ng L−1. Temporal separation of Hg0 formation was confirmed by gas chromatography coupled to atomic fluorescence spectrometry, also indicating that thiomersal is firstly degraded to ethylmercury before turning into Hg2+ and then into Hg0. The method was applied in the determination of thiomersal in vaccines and residual levels of Hg2+ and ethylmercury were also detected.

ce:section.ce:para: Thiomersal is an organomercuric compound used to prevent bacterial proliferation, guaranteeing safety and stability in cosmetological products, pharmaceuticals and vaccines [1,2]. As thiomersal degrades into thiosalicylic acid, dithiobenzoic acid and ethylmercury [3], there is a concern about its neurotoxicity [4] although studies [5,6] do not confirm direct connection between thiomersal and neuropathies [7]. According to the World Health Organization, there is no evidence that using thiomersal as a preserving agent in vaccines causes any harm as long as strict quantities are used [8]. For risk assessment purposes, precise and careful control of the dosage of thiomersal, at ultra-trace levels, in vaccines and pharmaceuticals is necessary.

High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) has been used to determine thiomersal either after derivatization with dithizone [9] or through detection of thiosalycilic acid after forced degradation [10]. HPLC has been coupled with inductively coupled plasma (ICP) mass spectrometry to determine thiomersal, through the 202Hg isotope [11]; fluorimetric determination has been made after photochemical convertion of thiomersal to thiosalycilic acid [12]; and with electrochemical detection either based on ionic suppression or coulometric detection [13]. The most sensitive methods to determine thiomersal (at μg L−1 and ng L−1 levels) are based on the decomposition of thiomersal, ultimately detecting cold vapor (CV) Hg0 with spectrometric methods. For instance, UV photo-degradation of thiomersal produced Hg2+ that was then reduced to Hg0 by ultrasound, in the presence of formic acid, before determination by atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), allowing quantification of 40 μg L−1 [14]. Atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS) enables detection at ng L−1 levels for thiomersal after Hg0 production, under UV, followed by chemical reduction of Hg2+ [15] and with possibility to couple with HPLC [16]. Heterogeneous photocatalysis has been used for the UV photodegradation of thiomersal using TiO2, dispersed in solution at acidic pH, transforming thiomersal directly into Hg0 determined by cold vapor AFS [17]. Visible radiation and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has been used to convert thiomersal into ethylmercury then to Hg2+ [18]. After chemical reduction of Hg2+ into Hg0, detection was made using a dedicated mercury portable atomic absorption spectrometer with a multi-pass detection cell, enabling LOD at the ng L−1 level [18].

GQDs have been used to photodegrade fuchsin dye [19], thiomersal [18], and methylene blue [20] under visible light. Literature also reports that Hg2+ combines with the carboxyl groups, at the edges of GQDs, promoting excitonic recombination [21,22]. The association GQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles has been studied as catalysts for photo-degradation of methylene blue (under UV) [23] and rhodamine B (under visible radiation) [24].

Recently, our laboratory has reported the photodegradation of thiomersal using GQDs as photocatalytic agents, under visible radiation, producing Hg2+ which was, in a second step, reduced with SnCl2 to be determined by CV-AAS achieving limit of detection (LOD) of 0.006 μg L−1) [18]. In addition, square wave voltammetry (SWV) was used to indirectly determine thiomersal using GQDs as a surface modifier of a glassy carbon electrode, exploring the synergistic effect between GQDs, visible radiation and the applied potential in producing an intense Hg oxidation peak during anodic scanning (LOD of 340 μg L−1) [29]. Mercury speciation based on the UV photodegradation of mercurial species (CH3Hg, CH3CH2Hg, Hg2+) was performed leading to Hg0 formation, detected by CV-AAS, at different releasing times due to the action of a GQDs-TiO2 nanocomposite [25]. The different kinetics for the reactions allowed speciation and determination ng L−1, indicating that is possible to perform the temporal separation of other mercurial species before determination by CV-AAS [25]. Characteristics of different analytical methods for the determination of thiomersal are summarized in Table S1.

In the present work, a new method for atomic absorption spectrometric determination of thiomersal is proposed, based on the efficient UV photo-degradation, assisted by GQDs-TiO2, that converts thiomersal into Hg0 without any further chemical steps using reducing agents. Speciation of thiomersal from possible traces of ethylmercury and Hg2+ was achieved due to the different temporal formation of Hg0 from these species. The method was used for the analysis of vaccines.

Ultrapure water (resistivity <18.2 MΩ cm) was used to prepare solutions. Thiomersal, thiosalicylic acid, tin chloride, and titanium (IV) dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticles were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Ethylmercury chloride (at 1 mg L−1), methylmercury chloride (at 1 mg L−1), mercury chloride (at 1 mg L−1), sodium tetra(n-propyl)borate derivatizing reagent and acetate buffer purified solution (pH 5.0) were obtained from Brooks Rand Instruments (USA). Citric acid, sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, ammonium hydroxide, hydrogen peroxide (30%), acetic acid (65% w/v), boric acid (99.8% w/w), phosphoric acid (85%, m/v), potassium chloride, sodium acetate and formic acid were purchased from Merck (Germany). The 1,4-dioxane was from Tedia (USA). PTFE syringe filters (0.45 μm) were obtained from Whatman (UK).

Mercury (cold vapor) was measured on a portable atomic absorption spectrometer, model RA-915 (Lumex, Russia), with a multipass optical length cell. The photo-reactor consisted of six sterilization mercury lamps (6 W each with main line at 253 nm) placed inside of a cylindrical PVC tube (20 cm diameter and 30 cm length) vertically mounted inside a wooden cabinet. Temperature inside the reactor was kept close to room-temperature by a fan, installed at the base of the reactor, and a magnetic stirrer was placed in the center of the reactor to keep agitation of the solution containing the dispersed photo-catalytic nanocomposite as reported in literature [25] and shown in Fig. S1. Stoppered quartz tubes were placed at the top of the magnetic stirrer with continuous slow agitation of the content, inside the tube, made with a small magnetic bar. The quartz tube was directly connected by means of flexible silicone hose, to the CV-AAS system. The Hg0, produced by photo-catalytic reaction, was washed (in NaOH 10% w/v) in the way to the spectrometer. In some experiments, when required, chemical reduction of Hg2+, using SnCl2, was made in the aqueous solutions accessory RP-92 (Lumex) connected to the CV-AAS system.

Thiomersal degradation kinetics and studies to further understand speciation were performed on a mercury speciation automated system (MERX-M from Brooks Rand Instruments) consisting of gas chromatography system with a mercury dedicated AFS detector. Mercurial derivatized species were separated on an isothermal GC column packed with 15% OV-3 (in Chromosorb W-AW) before thermal photo-decomposition into Hg0 [26].

A Perkin-Elmer model lambda 35 spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elemer, UK) was used to obtain extinction bands of the nanoparticles and nanocomposites dispersed in water. Voltammetric determination (comparative method) were made using a potentiostat μ-AUTOLAB Type III (Metrohm, The Netherlands) operating in the square-wave voltammetry (SWV) mode. A carbon glassy electrode modified with GQDs (ink of 10 μL deposited onto the surface) was used as the working electrode while a saturated Ag|AgCl(KCl(sat)) electrode and a Pt wire were used as reference and auxiliary electrodes respectively.

The nanocomposite was prepared using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and TiO2 nanoparticles following procedure described in a previous work [25]. Briefly, in a small beaker, 2.0 g of citric acid was melted in the presence of 60 mg of TiO2 nanoparticles. At about 200 °C, a white-orange molten liquid was formed and poured into 50 mL of NaOH solution (0.25 mol L- 1) keeping vigorous stirring for 24 h, at room temperature. The dispersion was centrifuged (at 11,200 RFC) for 30 min before collecting the solid pellet of GQDs−TiO2, at the bottom of the tube. The solid was dried, under vacuum, at 80 °C for 24 h.

Thiomersal stock solution (10 mg L−1) was prepared in water while intermediate solutions (2 μg L−1) were prepared by dilution the stock solution. Thiomersal standard solutions (from 50 to 600 ng L−1) were prepared from the intermediate solution and stored at 4 °C in the dark. Standard solutions of the mercurial species (methylmercury, ethylmercury and Hg2+) were prepared in amber volumetric flasks, from dilutions of the commercial standard solutions (1 μg L−1), keeping them refrigerated and protected from ambient light. Sodium tetra(n-propyl) borate solution (derivatizing agent) was prepared in KOH solution (1% v/v) and stored at 5 °C. The GQDs-TiO2 dispersions were prepared with 30 mg of the nanocomposite dispersed in 10 mL of water. Dispersions were purged with N2, in a glass washing bottle, for 20 min before use.

The average size of the GQDs-TiO2 was 19.5 nm, calculated from Scanning Transmission Electron Microscopy images. The dispersion of GQDs-TiO2 produced photoluminescence (345/456 nm) with quantum yield around 21%. Total carbon value in the original dispersion (before precipitation) was about 60 mg L−1. Other characteristics of the GQDs-TiO2 dispersion were recently reported by Miranda et al. [25].

Tauc plots, showing the relationship between (αhν)2 with the photon energy, are shown in Fig. S2. Bandgap of TiO2 (as P25 dispersed in water) was 3.16 eV, close to the one reported for TiO2 (∼ 3.2 eV for anatase and brookite, ∼3.0 eV for rutile) [27]. In contrast, the bandgap of the GQDs−TiO2 nanocomposite was 2.92 eV. The decreasing of the bandgap energy is probably due to the doping effect caused by GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of thiomersal using CV-AAS with photo-reaction mediated by GQDs−TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Aliquots of thiomersal solutions (samples after dilution or standard solutions) were transferred to a quartz tubes containing 5 mL of a solution (at pH 4.0) containing formic acid (1% m/v) and 150 μL of the GQDs-TiO2 dispersion). The mixture was kept under stirring during exposition to UV (16 min) and the produced Hg0 was continuously transported, by air flow (1 mL min −1) to the CV-AAS system, passing through a bubling cell containing NaOH solution (experimental conditions shown in Table 1 ). Proper blank measurements were made in the same way.

Vaccine samples, hepatitis B and diphtheria−tetanus, presented declared thiomersal concentrations of 100 μg mL−1, according to the vaccine instructions [28,29]. Each vaccine sample was diluted (100.000 times) with water before analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mercury speciation using a mercury analyzer with atomic fluorescence detection
ce:section.ce:para: Studies to understand photo-degradation kinetics were made using a mercury speciation analyzer with aliquots (0.10 mL) of standard solutions of thiomersal (or other mercurial species) mixed with dispersed GQDs-TiO2 prior to UV exposure in closed quartz tubes. Content was transferred to 40 mL amber flasks, with a septum cap, containing 20 mL of water, 0.2 mL of acetate buffer solution (pH 5.0) and 0.10 mL of sodium tetra(n-propyl)borate aqueous solution (at 1% v/v). Then, flasks were placed in the automatic sampler to be transferred to a glass cell, where it was bubbled with argon (312 mL min−1) that carried the volatile propylated species into the chromatographic module (column at 36 °C and argon flow mobile phase at 30 mL min−1). The separated propylated mercury species were thermally decomposed into Hg0 before quantification by fluorescence detection.

Square-wave voltammetry was used as described in literature [30] employing a working glassy carbon electrode modified with GQDs. Each of the vaccine samples was diluted in water (140 times) before a 3 mL aliquot was added to the electrochemical cell containing 3 mL of pH 4 Britton-Robinson buffer (at 0.02 mol L−1) and KCl 0.25 mol L−1. The electrochemical experiments were carried out inside a photo-reactor. After a few minutes of UV irradiation, a sequence of 20 voltammetric cycles (at 100 mV s−1 within ±300 mV) was applied to degrade thiomersal before voltammetric anodic scanning was made within ±1200 mV (at 25 Hz using 20 mV step potential and 40 mV pulse amplitude). The Hg/Hg2+ redox peak current was measured at +158 mV.

Photocatalytic activities of three different nanomaterials were compared in terms of efficiency in degrading thiomersal, aiming to prove the peculiar activities of the nanocomposite formed by GQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles. A volume of 200 μL of nanomaterial dispersion (prepared with 30 mg of nanomaterial in 5 mL of water) was added to a 5 mL solution of thiomersal (2 μg L−1) at pH 5 (adjusted with sulfuric acid solution). These solutions (containing the dispersed nanomaterials) were transferred to quartz test tubes and irradiated with either UV or visible light, under continuous agitation. Every 5 min (up to 60 min) the vapor content in tube headspace was sampled by turning on the pump of the CV − AAS system and simultaneously opening the valve that connect the quartz tube to the spectrometer, carrying the Hg0, through a continuous air flow, to be detected.

No matter the nanomaterials tested, the exposure to visible light produced no relevant conversion of thiomersal into Hg0. In contrast, under UV, the formation of Hg0 occurred as seen (in terms of relative signal) in Fig. 1 , probably after previous formation of ethylmercury. The study was not performed with GQDs alone as UV promotes their agglomeration, affecting their photocatalytic action and increasing turbidity of solution [18]. No matter the tested nanomaterial, the increasing of the measured Hg0 started at about 15 min until a maximum signal was observed, then becoming stable (continuous releasing of Hg0) for the remaining experiment time (up to 60 min). In the presence of GQDs-TiO2, the maximum Hg0 signal was achieved after 30 min of UV exposure. In contrast, when the dispersed nanomaterial was TiO2, 40 min of UV was necessary to lead to the maximum signal. Therefore, the GQDs-TiO2 was found to be more efficient in degrading thiomersal as the relative reaction rate, along the process until reaching stable signal, was about two times higher (1.4 ng min−1) than the one observed using TiO2 nanoparticles (0.7 ng min−1). Degradation rates were calculated as the sensitivity of response, within 15 and 30 min, of temporal profiles shown in Fig. 1A.

The maximum efficiency in degrading of thiomersal was about 38% in the presence of GQDs−TiO2 and about 16% when using TiO2, taking as reference the efficiency achieved in degrading ethylmercury using GQDs-TiO2 (obtained under conditions of [25]). Ethylmercury was used as reference since thiomersal probably degrades into ethylmercury before oxidation to Hg2+ that leads to the measured Hg0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of photodegradation using the GC-CV-AFS system with propylation of mercurial species
ce:section.ce:para: UV photo-degradation of thiomersal, in the presence of GQDs-TiO2, was monitored by gas chromatography coupled with atomic fluorescence spectrometry (GC − CV − AFS). Thiomersal solution (2 μg L−1) were exposed to UV in the presence of 200 μL of aqueous dispersion GQDs-TiO2 (30 mg of GQDs-TiO2 in 5 mL water). Aliquots of this mixture (equivalent to 300 pg of thiomersal) were collected before exposing to UV (0 min) and after exposing to UV for 5, 10, 15, 25 and 30 min. These aliquots were diluted in water and propylated to form volatile mercurial species to be separated in the GC column. Chromatograms can be seen in Fig. 1B, obtained from solutions UV irradiated at different times (time indicated in Fig. 1B in parenthesis) and showing the evolution profile of the different mercurial species can be seen. The solution non-exposed to UV presented a residual ethylmercury (about 8 pg), formed from natural degradation of thiomersal, (measured at 4.85 ± 0.02 min seen in Fig. 1B peak “a”) as well Hg0 (at 0.36 ± 0.02 min seen in Fig. 1B peak “i”). After 5 min of UV exposure, the peak corresponding to ethylmercury (Fig. 1B peak “b”) increased about 175% (to 22 pg) and after 10 min (in Fig. 1B peak “c”), a further increase occurred (about 475% compared to Fig. 1B peak “a”) equivalent to 46 pg of ethylmercury. Up to 10 min of UV irradiation, no peak corresponding the Hg2+ was detected and no increasing of residual Hg0 in sample was observed. When UV exposure was increased to 15 and 25 min, the amount of detected ethylmercury increased to about 100 pg (Fig. 1B peak “d”) and 147 pg (Fig. 1B peak “e”), corresponding to about 1150% and 1737% compared to the residual value measured before exposure to UV. Chromatographic peaks corresponding to the increasing formation of Hg2+ were also found at 7.78 ± 0.04 min: (Fig. 1B peak “f” (15 min UV) and Fig. 1B peak “g” (25 min UV). Besides, the Hg0 (at 0.36 ± 0.02 min) increased after exposed to UV for 15 min (Fig. 1B peak “j”) and 25 min (Fig. 1B peak “k”) due to further photo-degradation of ethylmercury derived from thiomersal. After 30 min of UV no ethylmercury was found as it was entirely transformed into Hg2+ (Fig. 1B peak “h”) and then to Hg0 (Fig. 1B peak “l”). Overall result showed that GQDs−TiO2 alone promoted photo-degradation of thiomersal leading to the formation of Hg0, showing better performance than GQDs alone, which requires a second step to further chemical reduce Hg2+ to the detected Hg0 as reported by Miranda-Andrades et al. [18]. Although less effective to degrade thiomersal than for degrading ethylmercury, experimental conditions for the photo-degradation of thiomersal, in the presence of GQDs−TiO2, still need adjustments to achieve best efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of photo-degradation adjusting pH and GQDs-TiO2 content for CV − AAS measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Thiomersal photo-degradation was studied using a central composite design with simultaneous variation of pH (2.0 to 12.0) of the solution and amount of dispersed GQDs−TiO2 (0,03 to 0.24 mg L−1) while keeping exposition to UV for 50 min. The dispersed GQDs-TiO2 was adjusted by adding different volumes of original synthesized nanocomposite dispersion (at 6 mg mL- 1) from 25 μL (0.03 mg L−1) to 200 μL (0.24 mg L−1) while the final pH was varied using volumes of 0.10 mol L−1 solutions of either NH4OH or H2SO4. Thiomersal concentration was kept at 2 μg L−1 and experimental design levels (code values in parentheses) for GQDs-TiO2, in terms of employed volume of dispersion, were: 20 μL (−√2); 43 μL (−1); 100 μL (0); 157 μL (+1) and 180 μL (+ √2) while pH levels were 2.0 (−√2); 3.5 (−1); 7 (0); 10.5 (+1) and 12 (+ √2). Nine authentic replicates were made at the central point (0;0) to estimate the experimental variance. The response surface can be seen in Fig. S3 and the model equation, based on the measured Hg0, was: y = 1081 (pH) - 133.9 (NC) + 16 pH 2–2.4 (NC)2 + 0.3 (pH) (NC), which showed strong influence of the amount of GQDs-TiO2, a significant influence of pH, a small influence of quadratic terms and absence of interaction between factors. Coefficient of variation at the central point was 2.3% and best degradation was achieved with added volumes of GQDs-TiO2 dispersion above 75 μL (0.09 mg L−1) at the pH range between 2.0 and 4.0.

Response surface indicated that higher quantities of GQDs-TiO2 could improve photo-degradation, therefore, a univariate study was made to find the appropriate volume of nanomaterial dispersion to be used (Fig. 2A). Maximum efficiency was observed with addition of volumes within 100 μL (0.12 mg L−1) and 200 μL (0.24 mg L−1) of the original GQDs-TiO2 dispersion (at 6 mg L−1). As this was found to be a robust parameter, 150 μL (0.18 mg L−1) was chosen. In addition, the adjustment of pH to 4.0 promoted best efficiency of thiomersal degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV exposure time for CV-AAS measurements
ce:section.ce:para: UV exposure was studied using thiomersal solutions (2 μg L−1) at pH 4.0 and containing 150 μL of the original GQDs-TiO2 dispersion (about 0.18 mg L−1 of nanocomposite). Solutions, under agitation, were exposed to UV, increasing times, before air flow was turned on and system valve opened to carry the produced Hg0 to the CV˗AAS system. As reference, the Hg0 produced by photo-degradation of a 1 μg L−1 solution of ethylmercury (under conditions of reference [25]: 9 min exposure to UV, 100 μL of GQDs−TiO2, formic acid 2% (v/v) and at pH 3.0) was used. It is known that hydrogen peroxide and formic acid help generating free radicals from semiconductor nanoparticles under UV, improving photo-degradation of mercurial species to Hg0 [31].

The ethylmercury concentration would be equivalent to the maximum obtained from thiomersal if efficiency of the first step of the photo-degradation is 100%. Maximum signal (measured as Hg0) was reached within 25 min of UV irradiation (Fig. 2B line ‘a’), remaining constant up to the longest UV exposure time tested (40 min). Thiomersal photo-degradation efficiency was 72% (calculated using the maximum signal obtained from photo-degradation of ethylmercury as reference). Photo-degradation was also made in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (1% v/v H2O2) and 1% v/v and formic acid (1% v/v) improving reaction efficiency to 95% (compared to the photo-degradation efficiency of ethylmercury) with maximum being achieved in only 18 min (Fig. 2B line ‘b’). Further studies using either only hydrogen peroxide or only formic acid revealed that most efficient photo-degradation could be achieved using only formic acid, at concentrations in the range between 1 and 2% v/v.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the relative proportion between GQDs-TiO2 and thiomersal in CV − AAS measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Photo-degradation efficiency of GQDs-TiO2 (0.18 mg L- 1) was studied measuring the Hg0 produced from UV photo-degradation of different concentrations of thiomersal (from 0.02 to 8 μg L−1). It was observed (Fig. S4) that the magnitude of the measured Hg0 was directly proportional to thiomersal concentration up to 6 μg L−1, when the production of Hg0 stabilizes, indicating saturation of nanocomposite active sites, as they are fully occupied, reaching a limit in induce photo-degradation.

In order to promote photo-degradation of thiomersal at concentrations higher than 6 μg L−1, larger amount of GQDs-TiO2 could be used. However, the chosen amount (0.18 mg L−1) was found suitable for the intended quantitative application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transformation of thiomersal into ethylmercury study using GC − CV − AFS
ce:section.ce:para: A kinetic study was carried out using thiomersal solution (300 ng L−1) aiming to evaluate photo-degradation of thiomersal to ethylmercury using GQDs−TiO2 (0.18 mg L−1). In this study, the reaction velocity was slowed down by using the original pH of the mixture (pH 5) in absence of formic acid. The Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic model [32] was used and experiments were made using the mercury speciation system (CV − GC − AFS), obtaining chromatograms at different UV exposure times (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12 and 15 min). After reaching specific UV exposition time, solutions were derivatized and the volatile mercurial species were transferred to the mercury speciation system.

Before UV exposition, residual ethylmercury was measured (Fig. 3A. peak ‘a’) and as the UV exposition occurred (from 2 to 12 min), the ethylmercury content increased (Fig. 3A. peaks ‘b’ to ‘g’ at tR = 4.85 min). After 15 min of UV, concentration of ethylmercury decreased as it degrades forming Hg2+ (Fig. 3A, peak ‘i’ at tR = 7.78 min) and Hg0 (Fig. 3A, peak ‘j’ at tR = 0.36 min).

The result fits a pseudo-first order model with Y = (0.181 L ng−1)X + 0.90 for the linear adjustment (R2 = 0.994) using log(ct/c0) versus time (where c0 and ct are, respectively, the initial concentrations of thiomersal and the one at a specific time). The apparent degradation rate constant (kapp = 0.29 min−1) was an approximation due to the small concentrations of thiomersal employed and calculated from the sensitivity of the log(ct/c0) versus time (Fig. 3B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of interferences using CV − AAS
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of other mercurial species (Hg2+, methylmercury, ethylmercury), at 1 μg L−1, were considered during the UV photo-degradation of thiomersal (2 μg L−1) in the presence of GQDs-TiO2. The study was made by obtaining the Hg0 temporal profile (measured by CV-AAS) formed from solutions containing each of the mercurial species but also with mixture of mercurial species by sampling in time intervals of 1 min of UV exposition. The presence of any of the tested mercurial species did not affect the production of Hg0 from photo-degradation of thiomersal since the different mercurial species are photo-degraded at different times under UV, with maximum evolution of Hg0 from Hg2+ at 5 min, ethylmercury at 9 min, methylmercury at 13 min and thiomersal at 16 min (Fig. 4 ). Photo-degradation of mercurial species resulted in temporally resolved profiles as the evolution of Hg0 from one specific species reaches maximum and returns to baseline before the Hg0 from the next mercurial species is released. Experiment was also made using mixture of mercurial species and the presence of other mercurial species does not affect the time profile area observed from thiomersal as the time profile areas from a standard solution of thiomersal is statistically equal to the one obtained in the presence of a mix of mercurial species.

Three analytical curves were obtained using thiomersal standard solutions (from 30 ng L−1 to 1000 ng L−1). A typical linear analytical curve (R2 = 0.999) presented the following equation: Y = (192.3 ± 0.1 L ng−1) X + (259.3 ± 8.2), where Y is the area of the temporal profile of Hg0 after 16 min of UV and X is the original concentrations of thiomersal. Limit of detection (15 ng L−1) and limit of quantification (28 ng L−1) were equivalent to thiomersal concentrations that produced Hg0 measurable intensity equivalent to 3 s and 10s, where s is the standard deviation (n = 6) of the lowest measurable thiomersal concentration in the analytical curve. Hg0 temporal profiles obtained from increasing concentrations of thiomersal are shown in Fig. 5 .

Instrumental precision (1.6%) was the coefficient of variation of the signal obtained from 10 aliquots of the same standard thiomersal solution (at 300 ng L−1). Intermediate precision (1.8%) was the coefficient of variation of analyte recoveries from analyses of ten different standard thiomersal solutions at 300 ng L– 1.

The precision of the developed method was also evaluated as intra-day and inter-day precision. For this, the indirect determinations of thiomersal (Hg0) were evaluated using the CV − AAS system, after photo-degradation, in the presence of GQDs−TiO2, of three replicates at three different concentrations of thiomersal (350 ng L−1; 750 ng L−1; and 1000 ng L- 1), in the same day and in three consecutive days. In both cases, precision obtained (relative standard deviation) was <1% (Table S2), ensuring that the proposed method is reproducible.

Quantification of thiomersal in vaccines (hepatitis B and diphtheria-tetanus multi-dose vials) was made with the proposed method. Both vaccines contains thiomersal at declared concentration of 100 μg mL−1 (vaccine instructions). Two sequential dilutions of samples were performed with water: first, a 50 μL aliquot of sample was diluted to 50 mL, then, a 0.5 mL aliquot of the diluted solution was further diluted to 50.00 mL. Subsequently, aliquots (2.5 mL) of these solutions were placed into quartz tubes containing acetate buffer (pH 4.0), formic acid and the dispersion of GQDs−TiO2 at conditions indicated in Table 1. After 16 min of UV, the found thiomersal concentrations (already corrected by the dilution factor to the value in the original sample) were (97.2 ± 1.6) % (n = 3) and (96.5 ± 3.0) % (n = 3) respectively for the hepatitis B and diphtheria-tetanus vaccines. Other components of the vaccine samples besides antigens (Al3+, Na+, K+, chloride, dihydrogen phosphate in the range of 0.1–0.5 mg L- 1) did not affect the photodegradation of thiomersal probably due to the large dilution factor (100.000 times) used.

The method also allowed estimation of residual concentrations of Hg2+ and ethylmercury (due to natural degradation of thiomersal) measured at the characteristic temporal profiles of these species. In hepatitis B vaccine, Hg2+ and ethylmercury were respectively 2.7 ± 0.2 μg L−1 and 3.2 ± 0.3 μg L−1 while residual concentrations in diphtheria-tetanus vaccine were respectively 2.3 ± 0.3 μg L−1 (Hg2+) and 3.8 ± 0.7 μg L−1 (ethylmercury).

Samples were also analyzed (three replicates) by voltammetry [29]. Recoveries were (95.7 ± 3.2) % and (94.8 ± 4.3) % respectively for hepatitis B and diphtheria-tetanus vaccines. A Student t-test (two-tailed; α = 0.05; d.f. = 4) indicated statistically equal results using both methods (texperimental = 0.832 < tcritical = 2.77 for hepatitis B vaccine and texperimental = 0.963 < tcritical = 2.77 for tetanus-diphteria B vaccine).

GQDs-TiO2 nanocomposite photo-catalytic properties enabled thiomersal degradation, with specific time resolved Hg0 absorbance profile, leading to the development of a method for quantification using CV-AAS. UV-induced photoreactions were carried out at pH 4.0 in the presence of formic acid as a source of free radicals. The doping of TiO2 nanoparticles with GDQs decreases bandgap favoring formation of oxygen free radical species that promote oxidation or organomercurial species (in this case thiomersal). As GDQs excitonic is also formed, after incidence of UV, electrons in promoted to the conduction band are prone to reduce Hg2+ into Hg0 [33]. In fact, photoluminescence band, measured from the GQDs−TiO2 in dispersion, decreases the presence of Hg2+ indicating the withdraw of electrons from conduction band thus preventing excitonic recombination (decreasing of photoluminescence intensity). The developed method is capable to detect thiomersal at the ng L−1 level through the detection of Hg0 released after about 16 min of UV exposition. The method is simple to use as the reactor vessel is directly coupled to the mercury dedicated spectrometer with a multipass optical cell. The study showed photo-degradation of thiomersal to ethylmercury prior to the formation of Hg2+ and then Hg0. The method was successfully applied to two types of vaccines.
Nothing to declare.

Jarol R. Miranda-Andrades: Investigation, methodology, formal analysis and writing-draft. Marlin J. Pedrozo-Penafiel: Formal analysis. Joseane A. Mendes: Formal analysis. Ricardo Queiroz Aucelio: Funding acquisition, supervision, writing-reiview and editing.

Miranda-Andrades thanks FAPERJ (E-201.951/2020–SEI-26003/002294) and Pedrozo-Penafiel acknowledges FAPERJ (E-26/202.408/2021).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.sab.2022.106477.
